title: STANDARD FOR STURGEON CAVIAR CXS 291-2010 Adopted in 2010. Amended in 2013, 2018.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR STURGEON CAVIAR CXS 291-2010 Adopted in 2010. Amended in 2013, 2018.
sections.summary: Document heading: title of the Codex Standard for Sturgeon Caviar (CXS 291‑2010), adopted 2010 and amended 2013 and 2018; no substantive provisions in this line.
Applies to granular sturgeon caviar produced from fishes of the Acipenseridae family.

Defines 'fish eggs' and 'caviar'; specifies caviar is made from Acipenseridae eggs of uniform size and characteristic colour, salted (3–5 g/100 g) for direct human consumption, and sets processing requirements including treatments to prevent pathogenic microorganisms, use of approved hormones for ovulated eggs, salting with food-grade salt, temperature controls for packaging/retail (+2 to +4 °C) and wholesale (0 to -4 °C), prohibition of freezing unless quality is preserved, permitted packagings, and restrictions on mixing species or lots when repackaging.

Defines 'fish eggs' as non-ovulated eggs separated from ovarian connective tissue (with ovulated eggs allowed from aquaculture) and 'caviar' as product made from Acipenseridae fish eggs treated with food-grade salt.

Specifies the product is prepared from eggs of sturgeon species in the Acipenseridae family (listed genera and hybrids), with eggs of about one size and characteristic colours, intended for direct human consumption and containing 3–5 g salt per 100 g in the finished product.

Requires treatments or conditions to prevent growth of spore and non-spore-forming pathogens; allows use of ovulated eggs only after hormonal induction with hormones approved by the competent authority and requires removal of adhesive layer and shell hardening, preparation by salting with food-grade salt, temperature controls (+2 to +4 °C for packaging/retail; 0 to -4 °C for wholesale), generally prohibits freezing unless quality is preserved, mandates packing in coated metal tins, glass jars or other food-grade containers, permits controlled repackaging from larger to smaller containers but prohibits mixing caviar from different sturgeon species or lots.

Requires caviar to be made from wholesome sturgeon eggs (genera as in Section 2.2), salt to be food-grade and meet Codex standards, and states final product conformity is determined by lot examination per Sections 8 and 10 and the provisions in Section 9.
Mandates caviar be prepared from eggs extracted from sound, wholesome sturgeons of the species/genera listed in Section 2.2 and fit for sale as fresh fish for human consumption.
Requires that salt used be food-grade quality and conform to all applicable Codex Standards.
States the product shall meet this Standard when a lot examined per Section 10 complies with provisions in Section 9, and that examinations shall be performed by the methods given in Section 8.
Permits use of acidity regulators, antioxidants and preservatives listed in Table 3 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) in foods conforming to this standard.

Requires compliance with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins (CXS 193-1995) and CAC maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs, and for caviar from ovulated eggs mandates that fish treatments (e.g., hormones) and resulting residue levels comply with the veterinary drugs provisions of the Code of Practice for Fish and Fishery Products (CXC 52-2003), including MRLs and withdrawal times.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codes of Practice, requires compliance with any established microbiological criteria, prohibits substances or residues in amounts hazardous to health, and requires the final product be free of foreign material that threatens human health.

Adds specific labelling requirements to the Codex General Standard: the food name rules (use of 'caviar' and customary names like Beluga/Ossetra/Sevruga or scientific/ID codes for species, hybrid designation, and 'caviar from ovulated eggs' wording), mandates storage instructions, requires facility registration code on repackaging, and requires lot and species markings on primary containers with certain mandatory particulars always on the container (others may be in accompanying documents if clearly linked).

Prescribes naming: the product shall be called 'caviar' (optionally with customary names like Beluga, Ossetra, Sevruga per country practice), species without common names may use identification code or scientific name, hybrids must be labeled 'hybrid' (parent species may be shown), and caviar from ovulated eggs must be labeled 'caviar from ovulated eggs' so as not to mislead consumers.
Requires labelling to include terms indicating appropriate time/temperature storage conditions.
When the product is repackaged the facility registration code must be identified on the labelling.

Each primary (non-retail) container must be labelled with lot number markings and the species; the name of the food, lot identification, name and address, and storage instructions must always appear on the container, though lot identification and name/address may be replaced by an identification mark if it is clearly identifiable with accompanying documents.

Sets sampling and examination rules: sampling of lots follows General Guidelines on Sampling (CXG 50-2004) with the primary container as the sample unit, net weight sampling per an appropriate Codex plan, and microbiological/pathogen sampling per CXG 21-1997; sensory/physical-chemical assessments must be by trained persons per CXG 31-1999, net weight is determined by subtracting empty container weight, and salt content is determined per the Standard for Salted Fish (CXS 167-1989).

Requires sampling of lots for product examination in accordance with CXG 50-2004 (primary container is the sample unit), net weight sampling per an appropriate Codex sampling plan, and sampling for pathogenic microorganisms and parasites per CXG 21-1997.

States that sensory and physical/chemical samples must be assessed by trained personnel in accordance with the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CXG 31-1999).
Specifies determination of net weight for each sample unit by subtracting the empty container weight from the total weight (net weight excludes packaging).
Requires salt content to be determined using the method set out in the Standard for Salted Fish and Dried Salted Fish of the Gadidae Family of Fishes (CXS 167-1989).

Defines that a sample unit is defective if it exhibits any of the properties set out in Sections 9.1–9.4, i.e., foreign matter, odour/flavour defects, consistency/condition defects, or objectionable matter.

Defines 'foreign matter' as any matter not derived from sturgeon eggs that is readily recognizable without magnification or present at levels (including when detected by magnification) indicating non‑compliance with good manufacturing and sanitation practices, provided it does not pose a health threat.

Defines odour and flavour defects as persistent, distinct objectionable odour and/or flavour indicative of decomposition, oxidation, feed-derived taste (for aquaculture), or contamination by foreign substances (e.g., fuel oil).

Lists consistency and condition defects for caviar, including hard or tenuous grain covers not easily chewable, rupture of outer membranes when separating grains, and presence of broken eggs or fluid.
Defines 'objectionable matter' as remnants of membranes and/or secreted fat present in finished caviar.

Specifies lot acceptance criteria: the number of defectives per Section 9 must not exceed the acceptable number in the applicable sampling plan (CXG 50-2004); the average net weight of sampled units must be at least the declared weight with no individual container below 95% of declared weight; and Sections 4–7 requirements on additives, contaminants, hygiene and labelling must be met.
Annex A provides identification codes of sturgeon species used in the standard (see accompanying table listing scientific names and three‑letter codes).

sections.section: Table .1 - IDENTIFICATION CODES OF STURGEON SPECIES
sections.summary: Table of sturgeon species identification codes mapping each scientific denomination to a three‑letter code and specifying hybrid code format as 'female code x male code' (e.g., YYY x XXX).
